name: David Hogg
subject: Our major announcement
disclaimer_text: PAID FOR BY LEADERS WE DESERVE, LEADERSWEDESERVE
body:
Did you see this, Peter?
# [](https://act.leaderswedeserve.com/go/852?refcode=em20250519-1&t=1&akid=2142%2E587540%2EdHAhBa)
Did you see our major announcement, Peter?
**Leaders We Deserve recently announced a $20 million effort to primary out-of-touch, ineffective incumbent House Democrats to replace them with young leaders who are ready to bring the urgency this moment requires.**
The reason we’re doing this is to get the Democratic Party into fighting shape — because it’s become abundantly clear that the base of our party feels like we are not doing anywhere near enough to stand up to Donald Trump.
**[The fight to elect the next generation of leaders starts today, Peter. Please donate $5 or whatever you can to support our urgent work.](https://secure.actblue.com/donate/em_ms_lwd_20250519_1_announcement?refcode=em20250519-2142&amount=5&refcode2=2142_587540_dHAhBa&akid=2142%2E587540%2EdHAhBa)**
**[Donate $5 »](https://secure.actblue.com/donate/em_ms_lwd_20250519_1_announcement?amount=5&refcode=em20250519-2142&refcode2=2142_587540_dHAhBa&akid=2142%2E587540%2EdHAhBa)**
We know that not everyone will agree with this decision — especially the D.C. consultants who’ve gotten rich by doing things the old way.
But we know people like you understand we have no other choice. Because we are done waiting.
Waiting for more of our students to get gunned down in math class.
Waiting for our planet to burn up before our eyes.
Waiting for someone else to save us — because nobody will save us, _except_ us.
**We are done waiting for out-of-touch, ineffective politicians to step aside.**
When the same politicians keep failing us year after year, it’s time for us to stand up and say get on board or get out of our way. Because if we’re being honest, _waiting_ looks a lot like _doing nothing._ And you and I both know we cannot afford to do nothing.
**[Donate $5 right now to elect the young, progressive leaders we deserve. When Gen Z and Millennial leaders step up to run for office, Leaders We Deserve will be right there to help them win because of generous supporters like you.](https://secure.actblue.com/donate/em_ms_lwd_20250519_1_announcement?refcode=em20250519-2142&amount=5&refcode2=2142_587540_dHAhBa&akid=2142%2E587540%2EdHAhBa)**
Thank you for being a part of this big moment,
David Hogg
Leaders We Deserve
* * *
**[Donate »](https://secure.actblue.com/donate/lwd-em-footer?refcode=em20250519-1&t=2&refcode2=2142_587540_dHAhBa&akid=2142%2E587540%2EdHAhBa)**
This email was sent to dpwillis67@gmail.com. Email is a critical way we communicate with grassroots supporters like you. However, if you would like to change your subscription preferences, here are some options:
* [Receive Fewer Emails](https://act.leaderswedeserve.com/signup/receive-fewer-emails/?t=3&akid=2142%2E587540%2EdHAhBa)
* [Update Your Information](https://act.leaderswedeserve.com/signup/update-your-information/)
* [Unsubscribe](https://act.leaderswedeserve.com/unsubscribe/unsubscribe/?t=4&akid=2142%2E587540%2EdHAhBa)

To give by check:
Leaders We Deserve
611 Pennsylvania Ave SE
#192
Washington, DC 20003
Email us: [info@leaderswedeserve.com](mailto:info@leaderswedeserve.com)
PAID FOR BY LEADERS WE DESERVE, LEADERSWEDESERVE.COM, NOT AUTHORIZED BY ANY CANDIDATE OR CANDIDATE'S COMMITTEE

name: Justice Democrats
subject: This is why we need more working-class representation
disclaimer_text: PAID FOR BY JUSTICE DEMOCRATS
body:
[1]Justice Democrats

Republicans in Congress are in the process of ramming through a budget
bill that takes from the poor and gives to the rich,
Peter.

If the GOP gets its way, they plan to slash nearly $1 TRILLION from
Medicaid, which will in turn kick 8.6 million people off their health
insurance.

[ https://secure.actblue.com/donate/jd-em-20250519?refcode=em20250519-36690&amounts=10%2C27%2C50%2C100%2C250%2C500%2C1000&amount=5&refcode2=36690_1203867_43V4II&akid=36690%2E1203867%2E43V4II ]Screenshot of a post on X from Ilhan Omar (@IlhanMN) that reads: 'What
we’ve been saying all along. Republicans are working to kick millions of
people off their health insurance all to fund tax cuts for billionaires.
Hands off Medicaid.' Below is an embedded post from Jake Sherman
(@JakeSherman) dated May 7, which reads: 'BREAKING -- A new CBO analysis
shows that several policies Republicans are considering for slashing
Medicaid spending would lead to millions of people losing health insurance
coverage. The analysis, requested by Democrats, says that per capita caps
for the...' followed by a 'Show more' link.

According to Data for Progress, there is not a single congressional
district in the country where more than 15 percent of voters support
Medicaid cuts. This is a deeply unpopular move by Republicans to make
their millionaire and billionaire friends all the more wealthy at our
expense.

It’s not just Medicaid on the chopping block. As it stands, the current
bill would also cut other health, nutrition, education, housing
assistance, and clean energy funding — all to pay for tax cuts for the
wealthiest few and pump more money into the military and Trump’s
deportation force.

This budget is the embodiment of Trump and MAGA Republicans’ devotion to
corporations and the billionaire class, while throwing poor and
working-class families under the bus.

The Squad and other Justice Democrats’ members in Congress have been
leading the call against these potential cuts to the programs and services
that help our families and communities get by.

halls of power, the stronger our movement will be to stop bad budgets like
this. Fuel our 2026 election work by chipping in $5 or more
today.

subject: Linda McMahon just dropped a bombshell on House Democrats - now it's our turn to fight back
disclaimer_text: Paid for by Elect Republicans and not authorized by any candidate or candidate’s committee
body:
Linda McMahon just took a bold stand for our children - now it's our turn to stand with her, Peter.

| [ ](https://links.republicanemails.org/r?u=S3_zm1JcyYzuhKh2Rp619n25bc9KSHiZdjEVEE_fEsbagFWrDmAka3z-iqq_TPbw7aEvTy52PGOjpgI-q4EUY-R_XajjN688kpdwfgtXoojf8YisQeAw1D3TKXTuAekUYiO_dHID07Nlcsaax60JnnTt2OtMWkehDGiGO60Gor935eCfbxFyS7TSUQwwaTA8o5AEN7DALVo1-JCwU9gxidhcj_l39J6kzxIMaqZ_HiCP0dAHB3yscDMApws8HvavYikqfWL5c4QDsZf-OYE7ZxmgaJknKJ2aRcJqAYNY313tEUK2iEanSNvr7DhRinUNxEmSFbI-SgAA6rvRsHqTlIdmaYhHjCZKIUaWWN7ERKvEQooANVRS8dZ2G1Rr9SSR6qvXQSgRZIVA-7XN3-8vdhtJXKo-fd0a0x_xb62tacmK6DWP1UOr-Qz4k7QHuP57YlLA1XhJhAaAJqtmUSVT9HaauHYR7qrlfqLOgA2djZwsjO0Bh_T959Hy7sT6tMJLDTlbUC01DYNFr4PjpJiqSA&e=1a1fe10c9303676f5a8934f3586944e0&utm_source=electrepublicans&utm_medium=email&utm_campaign=em_197d3d7acdcfb23453964487c59&n=1) Dear Peter, As I'm sure you're aware, Education Secretary Linda McMahon recently dropped a bombshell on House Democrats, delivering a powerful message of nonpartisanship at their own press conference. She reminded them that it's about the children, not partisan politics. **[This bold move shows us that Republicans are fighting for what's right, even in the face of Democratic obstruction.](https://links.republicanemails.org/r?u=S3_zm1JcyYzuhKh2Rp619n25bc9KSHiZdjEVEE_fEsbagFWrDmAka3z-iqq_TPbw7aEvTy52PGOjpgI-q4EUY-R_XajjN688kpdwfgtXoojf8YisQeAw1D3TKXTuAekUYiO_dHID07Nlcsaax60JnnTt2OtMWkehDGiGO60Gor935eCfbxFyS7TSUQwwaTA8o5AEN7DALVo1-JCwU9gxidhcj_l39J6kzxIMaqZ_HiCP0dAHB3yscDMApws8HvavYikqfWL5c4QDsZf-OYE7ZxmgaJknKJ2aRcJqAYNY313tEUK2iEanSNvr7DhRinUNxEmSFbI-SgAA6rvRsHqTlIdmaYhHjCZKIUaWWN7ERKvEQooANVRS8dZ2G1Rr9SSR6qvXQSgRZIVA-7XN3-8vdhtJXKo-fd0a0x_xb62tacmK6DWP1UOr-Qz4k7QHuP57YlLA1XhJhAaAJqtmUSVT9HaauHYR7qrlfqLOgA2djZwsjO0Bh_T959Hy7sT6tMJLUQ8KtbfIVDK5u5tpkMOVYQ&e=1a1fe10c9303676f5a8934f3586944e0&utm_source=electrepublicans&utm_medium=email&utm_campaign=em_197d3d7acdcfb23453964487c59&n=2)** But we can't let up - we need your help to keep pushing back against the liberal agenda. With the recent proposed budget cuts threatening education and research programs, it's more important than ever that we stand strong and united. body:
Let’s call this “big, beautiful” bill what it really is.
#
Let’s call this “big, beautiful bill” what it really is, Peter.
**A scam for the American people. A boon for billionaires.**
Any politician who says otherwise is lying — and they know it.
They are actively working to cut health coverage for millions of Americans. They want to reduce food assistance for working families and hungry children. They want to strip our communities for parts, all so the billionaires bankrolling their careers can get even richer.
**Enough is enough.**

clean_body: | | | ****The party of life? The most cruel and corrupt president in American history, Donald Trump is halting crucial medical research aimed at preventing heart defects in babies. This action not only stifles innovation but also threatens the health and wellbeing of countless infants who could benefit from life-saving advancements. Research funded by the National Institutes of Health was making significant strides in understanding and preventing congenital heart defects—one of the leading causes of infant mortality. We must take a stand against this negligence. It’s time to elect Democrats who are committed to defending life and investing in medical research that saves lives. If you've saved your payment information with ActBlue Express, your donation will go through immediately. ** By cutting off this vital funding, the Trump administration has jeopardized the future of countless children, prioritizing political agendas over the health of our nation's most vulnerable. The irony is stark: the so-called "party of life" is now responsible for putting more lives at risk, leaving families in despair and without options. The cruelty of the Trump regime is literally heartbreaking. Together, we can reclaim the promise of life. -Able Dems | elects Democrats with disabilities. We fight to protect accessible voting, expand health care and Social Security, and equality for all. To donate by check, send to: Able Dems PAC c/o Pocketbook Strategies 1110 N Virgil Ave #375 Los Angeles, CA 90029 \- \- Paid for by Able Dems PAC and not authorized by any candidate or candidate's committee. **Peter, the Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE) is on the far-left’s chopping block!** They hate that DOGE is uncovering all their waste, fraud, and abuse, including **MILLIONS in DEI spending!** _They’ve already rallied thousands of left-wing protestors across the country calling for the death of DOGE._ And the sad truth is that if millions don’t speak out, they could pressure Congress to _**SHUT DOGE DOWN FOREVER.**_ We must show everyone that the Silent Majority approves of DOGE because they want their tax dollars to be used efficiently and responsibly. [_**Will you take the Official D.O.G.E. Survey in the next 15 MINUTES and let us know where you stand on DOGE?**_](http://links.newsletter.conservativedirect.com/s/c/6qESI6Gl7nd6rx94MZ8Me_G-auIJ_eYyuutrNaN9J903twf5AuwTEa_v6TMKuzR4oe7szC59Dyg4BtPUr9PyXlO5neoUBpkMIJB12hphCHuCdMwrLBNHz_SzUF9r8PB0sBwqREgyoWbsD_rJg8DdsKBPFTC3QTI4Zvb3W9uNW3C_CLUkoF_6CRyOXbusj15O6nZ7g72YDh55rdcJVT0FOs4xefBaQfnzx2jJGGt0jO1HDnHfpD4w1VjbmUoycKqN2ni06uVF0B6a_-O6aUOlbbqVY48kWtLhW0OZFrGDBwvDujf0GSrijKa3Yo3EJPNKiV1DZo2SOMPcOH0HRK9auK3DRGL_98eNYRiov_rBIXMVp-2VHSb403iTW-T742i2uvBEYR3EbSSPAy8DMkr2V06Hj1PzbgRcpbW6B1QMX7hA-ZBXWAcTVxjK/jbwT9nEzNAZ62y_bfD8h2UXkEQZKI_Yr/9)

name: Shawnna Bolick
subject: May 19th Capitol Update & Invite
disclaimer_text: PAID FOR BY VOTE BOLICK
body:
| | | [ ](https://bolickforarizona.us7.list-manage.com/track/click?u=3214fdbd528e32d06cf84fc86&id=d01fc3d8cb&e=a7ab36849c)
---
| | Good afternoon!

Even though the legislature took a brief pause to work on a compromise for the Proposition 123 referral and of course a state budget, I have been still working for my constituents. The Senate is set to return early next week to wrap up some more work. Luckily for all of us, not all the original 1,806 bills that were introduced received a hearing or continue to move through the process.

There are still a few of my bill awaiting action in the House, including

**SB1271** ballots; count; identification number
**SB1507** independent corrections ombudsman; auditor general
**SB1660** sex offender management board; fund
**SCR1027** elections; foreign contributions; certification
**SB1226** ACJC; continuation
**SB1229 **planning; home design; restrictions; prohibition
**SB1405** mental health transition program; extension
**SB1658 **animal cruelty; failure to treat

_From a 15-person Passenger Van to the Vineyard_

Living in Arizona, water will always be a huge issue for our state. In 2022, voters in the Willcox water basin rejected a ground water management plan regulating groundwater use. Then, late last year the Director of the Department of Water Resources issued an order to set up the [Willcox AMA](https://bolickforarizona.us7.list-manage.com/track/click?u=3214fdbd528e32d06cf84fc86&id=4fb9d872b3&e=a7ab36849c). Over a week ago, I joined a group of bipartisan legislators on an Arizona Wine Growers Association tour to the Willcox area to learn directly from agribusinesses and wine producers in the region about their water future.

Wine tourism contributes to our state’s economy and continues to grow, but they might not be able to continue to expand their operations or wine production due to a lack of certainty about their naturally low water-use crop even though grapes are considered a high value crop. Between 2011 and 2023, wine visitor spending increased from over $31 million to over $241 million.

There is still some time this legislative session to potentially address this issue since there are still a few water-related bills being finalized. By and large, the legislature could help ensure the continued growth of our state’s wine production in our growing economy. Also, if you like to consume [Arizona wines](https://bolickforarizona.us7.list-manage.com/track/click?u=3214fdbd528e32d06cf84fc86&id=bdc9d444a5&e=a7ab36849c) you can always take a day trip, or you can visit one of the many tasting rooms across the state.

_You're Invited to an Upcoming Event _

Join me tomorrow morning, May 20th at 7:30am at the Arizona State Capitol for a [Rally for Pets and a press conference for Senate Bill 1658 to stop animal cruelty](https://bolickforarizona.us7.list-manage.com/track/click?u=3214fdbd528e32d06cf84fc86&id=2a1d81274e&e=a7ab36849c). For the past two legislative sessions the Arizona Humane Society and our law enforcement professionals have come down to the Capitol to advocate for this bill. To learn more, please visit Arizona Humane Society’s website for [events](https://bolickforarizona.us7.list-manage.com/track/click?u=3214fdbd528e32d06cf84fc86&id=62c5709c4b&e=a7ab36849c). Together, let’s get this bill across the line this year and stand for our pets. This bill was originally introduced last legislative session to give law enforcement tools needed in the aftermath of a disgusting hoarding situation that took place in [Chandler](https://bolickforarizona.us7.list-manage.com/track/click?u=3214fdbd528e32d06cf84fc86&id=22a8821571&e=a7ab36849c) in 2023. Sadly, the Maricopa County Sheriff's Office have had a few more animal cruelty cases earlier this year, including one in [Gila Bend](https://bolickforarizona.us7.list-manage.com/track/click?u=3214fdbd528e32d06cf84fc86&id=7f558ff4ff&e=a7ab36849c) and one in my [district](https://bolickforarizona.us7.list-manage.com/track/click?u=3214fdbd528e32d06cf84fc86&id=7b50d7bbd2&e=a7ab36849c).

_Bolick in the News_

[https://pro.stateaffairs.com/az/legislative-report/legislature/sen-shawnna-bolick-animal-cruelty](https://bolickforarizona.us7.list-manage.com/track/click?u=3214fdbd528e32d06cf84fc86&id=4abcdf9cec&e=a7ab36849c)

[https://pro.stateaffairs.com/az/yellow-sheet/legislature/arizona-department-of-corrections](https://bolickforarizona.us7.list-manage.com/track/click?u=3214fdbd528e32d06cf84fc86&id=7c90829dd9&e=a7ab36849c)

[https://azcapitoltimes.com/news/2025/05/17/dont-get-locked-out-of-the-american-dream-by-legislators/](https://bolickforarizona.us7.list-manage.com/track/click?u=3214fdbd528e32d06cf84fc86&id=d204e80aba&e=a7ab36849c)

[https://azcapitoltimes.com/news/2025/05/16/triple-homicide-in-state-prison-prompts-legislators-probe/](https://bolickforarizona.us7.list-manage.com/track/click?u=3214fdbd528e32d06cf84fc86&id=566972eade&e=a7ab36849c)

[https://pro.stateaffairs.com/az/yellow-sheet/governor/governor-katie-hobbs-greer-fire](https://bolickforarizona.us7.list-manage.com/track/click?u=3214fdbd528e32d06cf84fc86&id=25162a3db8&e=a7ab36849c)

_Campaign Information_

Do you reside in one of Legislative District 2’s zip codes?

85022 85023 85024 85027 85029 85032 85050 85053 85085 85308 85310 85331

If you answered yes and you are a registered Republican or an Independent voter, [**please take a minute to sign my online nomination petition**](https://bolickforarizona.us7.list-manage.com/track/click?u=3214fdbd528e32d06cf84fc86&id=da02f2e378&e=a7ab36849c) ensuring my name appears on the Republican primary ballot. After session my team, will actively work to accomplish our goal, but I’d surely appreciate your help. Today, you can use your driver's license to log on and sign my online nomination petition.

Thank you for your continued support!

Take care,
Shawnna

name: Sarah Kline
subject: ACTION ALERT: Letter to the Editor Opportunity
body:
| | | [](https://click.everyaction.com/k/107987977/546487195/-1715290440?nvep=ew0KICAiVGVuYW50VXJpIjogIm5ncHZhbjovL3Zhbi9BVi9BVkxDVi8xLzcwODc3IiwNCiAgIkRpc3RyaWJ1dGlvblVuaXF1ZUlkIjogImViZjViNGVjLWUzMzQtZjAxMS1hNWYxLTYwNDViZGE5ZDk2YiIsDQogICJFbWFpbEFkZHJlc3MiOiAiZHB3aWxsaXM2N0BnbWFpbC5jb20iDQp9&hmac=ckHikpOHpNVqI2eQcpDnSrM8Vcr2MkOrckGbpSK0Zyo=&emci=90c7e15f-d534-f011-a5f1-6045bda9d96b&emdi=ebf5b4ec-e334-f011-a5f1-6045bda9d96b&ceid=2107230)
---
Dear Peter, Climate change is here, and our communities are already feeling the impacts. That’s why OLCV has joined [**Building Resilience**](https://click.everyaction.com/k/107987978/546487196/-1480106498?nvep=ew0KICAiVGVuYW50VXJpIjogIm5ncHZhbjovL3Zhbi9BVi9BVkxDVi8xLzcwODc3IiwNCiAgIkRpc3RyaWJ1dGlvblVuaXF1ZUlkIjogImViZjViNGVjLWUzMzQtZjAxMS1hNWYxLTYwNDViZGE5ZDk2YiIsDQogICJFbWFpbEFkZHJlc3MiOiAiZHB3aWxsaXM2N0BnbWFpbC5jb20iDQp9&hmac=ckHikpOHpNVqI2eQcpDnSrM8Vcr2MkOrckGbpSK0Zyo=&emci=90c7e15f-d534-f011-a5f1-6045bda9d96b&emdi=ebf5b4ec-e334-f011-a5f1-6045bda9d96b&ceid=2107230), to promote healthy resilient communities that run on affordable clean energy **AND** [**Move Oregon Forward**](https://click.everyaction.com/k/107987979/546487197/-1295365595?nvep=ew0KICAiVGVuYW50VXJpIjogIm5ncHZhbjovL3Zhbi9BVi9BVkxDVi8xLzcwODc3IiwNCiAgIkRpc3RyaWJ1dGlvblVuaXF1ZUlkIjogImViZjViNGVjLWUzMzQtZjAxMS1hNWYxLTYwNDViZGE5ZDk2YiIsDQogICJFbWFpbEFkZHJlc3MiOiAiZHB3aWxsaXM2N0BnbWFpbC5jb20iDQp9&hmac=ckHikpOHpNVqI2eQcpDnSrM8Vcr2MkOrckGbpSK0Zyo=&emci=90c7e15f-d534-f011-a5f1-6045bda9d96b&emdi=ebf5b4ec-e334-f011-a5f1-6045bda9d96b&ceid=2107230), to promote a safe, sustainable, climate friendly, and accountable transportation package. Both the Building Resilience campaign and Move Oregon Forward campaign have seen some buzz about the Energy Affordability package and the Transportation package. **Our voices can still be heard, as legislators make their final decisions.** We are looking for community members, who'd be interested in writing a Letter to the Editor to their local newspaper or another publication. The 2025 legislative session is coming to a close, and that means legislators are negotiating the energy affordability bills and transportation package! Oregonians like you can make a huge difference by sharing your clean energy, climate resilience, and transportation stories. **We know that when it comes to rising costs of living, and climate resilience everyone has a story to share. ** **Are you interested in writing an LTE focused on transportation or energy affordability? If so, Building Resilience and Move Oregon Forward are co-hosting an LTE workshop on May 22nd, from 6-7pm.** **EVENT: Building Resilience and Move Oregon Forward LTE Workshop****WHEN: Thursday, May 22nd from 6:00 PM - 7:00 PM****WHERE: Zoom, link provided to RSVPs**

clean_body: | | | Dear Peter, Climate change is here, and our communities are already feeling the impacts. That’s why OLCV has joined , to promote healthy resilient communities that run on affordable clean energy AND , to promote a safe, sustainable, climate friendly, and accountable transportation package. Both the Building Resilience campaign and Move Oregon Forward campaign have seen some buzz about the Energy Affordability package and the Transportation package. Our voices can still be heard, as legislators make their final decisions. We are looking for community members, who'd be interested in writing a Letter to the Editor to their local newspaper or another publication. The 2025 legislative session is coming to a close, and that means legislators are negotiating the energy affordability bills and transportation package! Oregonians like you can make a huge difference by sharing your clean energy, climate resilience, and transportation stories. We know that when it comes to rising costs of living, and climate resilience everyone has a story to share. Are you interested in writing an LTE focused on transportation or energy affordability? If so, Building Resilience and Move Oregon Forward are co-hosting an LTE workshop on May 22nd, from 6-7pm. EVENT: Building Resilience and Move Oregon Forward LTE WorkshopWHEN: Thursday, May 22nd from 6:00 PM - 7:00 PMWHERE: Zoom, link provided to RSVPs Letters to the Editor are great ways to show legislators in a public way that we support climate resilience, for both transportation and energy! We hope to see you there! Please reach out to organizers@buildingresilience.org with any questions, and don't forget to RSVP before it's too late! Warmly, Sarah Kline (she/they) OLCV Communications & Membership Coordinator | | | | | ---|---|--- Oregon League of Conservation Voters 1110 SE Alder Suite 220 Portland, OR 97214 United States If you believe you received this message in error or wish to no longer receive email from us, please .

Fellow Conservative,

As a combat-proven commander, I know the early stages of any campaign can set the tempo for an entire battle.

I started my campaign to flip the country’s MOST contested Senate seat (Georgia) **RED** last Monday. **The money we raise in this first week will have a huge impact on how this campaign takes shape.**

_**My Democrat opponent, Jon Ossoff, has already shattered fundraising records, raising more than $11 million in the first three months of the year**_. No doubt, he’s already raised millions more, too.

We must come out swinging in the **_first week_. It’s the only way to guarantee our campaign has what it takes to stay on offense.**

* **TOP NEWS**

"**Relatively quiet Jeep Weekend is wrapping up Sunday after 275 arrests amid massive police patrol** ," Houston Chronicle's **Caroline Wilburn** \-- "Almost 300 people have been arrested on Bolivar Peninsula since police put into effect their Jeep Weekend enforcement plan Tuesday.

On Sunday, as [a relatively quiet Jeep Weekend wound down](https://substack.com/redirect/45cd5fb7-6fc4-4bdb-aee0-72ced85ae7d7?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg), the Galveston County Sheriff's Office said a total of 275 people had been arrested and 42 guns recovered over the past six days, according to spokesperson **Zach Davidson**.

The Harris County Sheriff's Office, which had been helping manage the event since Friday, began breaking down its equipment and packing up on Sunday. The sheriff's office didn't immediately respond to a request for comment.
[BECOME A PAID SUBSCRIBER: $7/mo](https://substack.com/redirect/7ff50702-dff1-43d9-921e-531aa5efa313?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)
Beaches along Bolivar Peninsula were mostly deserted Saturday, the [result of increased law enforcement in the area](https://substack.com/redirect/99fc63c5-2ef2-49b3-93a7-737f3c2b71ee?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) as Galveston County Sheriff Jimmy Fullen looked to put an end to the typically rowdy and chaotic event. More than a dozen agencies aided in patrolling the peninsula's beaches and Texas State Highway 87, where police checkpoints were set up to keep guns and violence off the beach.
[BECOME A PAD SUBSCRIBER: $70/yr](https://substack.com/redirect/7ff50702-dff1-43d9-921e-531aa5efa313?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)
"If you control the traffic you control the whole event and that's exactly what we did," Fullen said Saturday. He made security during Jeep Weekend a key part of his campaign run last year, vowing to put an end to the violence and chaos of previous years.

It's not yet known how many people made the trip to Bolivar Peninsula for this year's event. But estimates put last year's crowd at about 100,000." [Hou Chron](https://substack.com/redirect/d48956f7-4336-4081-bac9-f4f09451a582?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) ($)

* * *
"**Power restored at a Federal Aviation Administration tower at Hobby Airport, officials say,** " Houston Chronicle's **Michael Garcia** \-- "Power has been restored to a Federal Aviation Administration tower at Hobby airport, after an hour-long outage, officials say.

A power outage at the tower, from which the flow of aircraft on and around the airport is managed, was reported around 1:15 p.m., a spokesperson with Houston Airports said. Backup generators immediately kicked in and kept the facility operable until power was restored at about 2:15 p.m., the spokesperson said." [Hou Chron](https://substack.com/redirect/19e2f88f-8dc1-41fd-89fa-4d0a598e9654?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) ($)

"**Austin police: 1 arrested for allegedly shooting Infowars writer while trying to steal Kia** ," Austin American-Statesman's**Tyler J. Davis** \-- "Austin police have announced an arrest connected to the killing of an South Austin man who wrote for Infowars.

**Eloy Adrian Camarillo** , 17, was arrested April 30, according to a [Thursday news release from the Austin Police Department,](https://substack.com/redirect/3fa19ad2-2186-46c8-8edd-ff9e6446805a?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) in relation to the death of**Jamie White** , and is being charged with capital murder, police say.

Camarillo is the first person arrested in the March 9 death of White, 36. White was allegedly trying to stop his car from being stolen at the Chandelier Apartments in the 2300 block of Douglas Street, when he was shot, according to previous information from police.

Police said multiple pieces of evidence, including surveillance camera footage, helped investigators narrow onto Camarillo. "Thank you to the community members who submitted tips through the Capital Area Crime Stoppers Program. ... Your willingness to come forward with information played a critical role in ensuring justice and maintaining the safety of our neighborhoods," the Facebook post announcing the arrest reads.

APD detective **Jason Jones** , who was leading the investigation, said in a March news conference that either one or multiple individuals were trying to steal White's Kia from the parking lot of his apartment complex when the writer "interrupted" the burglars.

White was then shot in the parking lot, where authorities found him. He was pronounced deceased at 12:19 a.m. at a local hospital.

Kia vehicles have drawn national attention for the past[ several years because of a spike in theft increases due to poor security measures.](https://substack.com/redirect/24f674e0-260b-422c-ab92-615d437dd3c0?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) Police also cited Kia's believed ease of accessibility as a factor, [though the company denied those charges to the Statesman.](https://substack.com/redirect/7dc89ec0-bb05-4439-9cc1-b9f7910d52dd?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)

It is unclear Camarillo has an attorney." [AAS](https://substack.com/redirect/8f914720-4dee-443f-afb8-2d150dd435af?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) ($)

**Kirk Bohls column** : "**Making a pitch for an MLB team in Austin. Three entrepreneurs setting the stage to land a franchise,** " via Houston Chronicle -- "**Matt Mackowiak** has a plan.

The long-time Austin political strategist and public relations guru seeks to [bring a major-league baseball team](https://substack.com/redirect/05039c72-e265-4ecc-b376-7b90e4249f98?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) to the biggest, untapped professional sports market in the country as he has launched a highly skilled, collaborative team looking to galvanize the community and make a bold bid for an expansion franchise.

That's right.

He'd like to have butts in the seats of a plush, 40,000-seat, brand new stadium with a retractable roof somewhere in eastern Travis County watching Major League Baseball in the 2030s.

Just imagine it.

In the season opener in 2031, Austinites could go buy a hot dog and brewski, settle into their front-row seat behind home plate and watch the New York Yankees play the Austin Bats.

Talk about your real-life field of dreams.

But these are the dreams of chief executive officer Mackowiak and his co-founding partners, chief operating officer Derrik Fox and president Dustin Byington, just less fantasy and more factual.

Fox is an upstate New York native who moved to Austin several years ago and has been to Cooperstown 40 minutes away from his grandparents' home in upstate New York to checked out the baseball Hall of Fame more times than he can count. He brings a wealth of experience in sales and marketing.

Byington is a 40-year-old who grew up in a family of San Diego Padres season ticket holders and saw about 50 home games a year since he was a sixth-grader. The one-time left tackle for Columbia's football team moved to Austin with his wife in 2013. He is a serial entrepreneur who offers incredible finance and private equity know-how and charged with raising capital.

They can envision major league baseball in Austin maybe as soon as 2030, but more likely the following year.

There's just a few little nagging details that need to be ironed out first between now and then:

> Like a major investor worth billions who loves baseball.
> Like a prime location for a brand new stadium easily accessible to many.
> Like an announcement from major-league baseball [to formalize the expansion process and timeline](https://substack.com/redirect/05039c72-e265-4ecc-b376-7b90e4249f98?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg).
> Like tremendous community outreach that fully impresses 30 MLB owners that Austin deserves a team.
> Like millions of dollars to set up infrastructure, hire experts, trigger a groundswell of support and finance their pitch to MLB.

Yeah, that's pretty much it.

Check all those boxes, and Austin is on its way to becoming the 31st or 32nd major-league baseball team. And, yes, that prospect is long overdue.

"It's going to be a long journey," Byington said. "I'm not saying we're running downhill now, but we've sure got a heck of a lot of momentum and we're picking up speed."

For sure, it figures to be a bumpy, full-contact road full of problems and hurdles. And Mackowiak, a Pittsburgh native who still hasn't gotten over Atlanta's Sid Bream's slide into home plate to deprive his beloved Pirates of a World Series berth in 1992. "Still traumatic to this day," said Mackowiak, who modeled his own game after smooth Bucs center fielder**Andy Van Slyke** before turning to basketball and playing for Westwood High School's 5A state semifinalist.

Mackowiak graduated from the University of Texas, where he was a Texas Cowboy and owns a corporate public relations and business advisory firm. He, Fox and Byington have laid the groundwork for an aggressive bid, received counsel from a former MLB deputy commissioner, raised almost half a million dollars in seed money and researched eight to 10 potential sites for a ballpark. They formed the Austin Baseball Commission last June as an LLC and converted to a limited partnership in April.

Yeah, they've been busy.

"It's time," Mackowiak said. "This is an opportunity to make Austin a baseball mecca. We have the power to add to the University of Texas story with its Omaha history. Clearly the arrow is pointed up. All we've wanted is to ensure Austin puts the strongest bid possible to get one of the two expansion slots."

To be clear, there's no guarantee ANY expansion slots are open at this time. However, MLB commissioner **Rob Manfred** is on the record as saying he'd like to expand from the current 30 to 32 teams before his term expires in 2029, which is only about 250 Aaron Judge home runs away and will get here faster than we think.

**Why Austin?**

Mackowiak and Fox first conceived of the idea in February 2024 and conducted a four-month, due-diligence phase that was publicly launched on the Friday of All-Star weekend last July in Arlington.

Byington joined forces last fall, and the three of them have since participated in more than 500 meetings and couldn't be more pumped about an event June 8 at the Austin Beer Garden Brewery with live music and more news of the progress.

This is a bootstrap operation that has grown exponentially to its current place, but the hardest pieces to the puzzle remain. They've created a glitzy, colorful website at [atxmlb.com](https://substack.com/redirect/6f58860e-aaba-48ad-b6da-3287649d91cc?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) and are looking to raise $1 million by Labor Day and $2 million by the end of the year.

They've fallen in love with the name Austin Bats, thanks in part to the largest colony of Mexican free-tailed bats whose numbers summer under the Congress Avenue Bridge and the obvious baseball connection. They haven't trademarked the name -- a riff on that of a defunct Austin minor league hockey team -- and would probably have to reach a financial settlement with the Louisville Bats, the Triple-A club of the Reds, or find a different mascot.

"The only areas that we've been slow out of the gate has been the investment piece," Fox said. "We made a lot of progress markers in early fall of last year. We got in a Ferrari to go from Point A to Point B. Others have been like driving a Ford Taurus."

It's true that there is no mandate to expand yet, and a looming fight over a new collective bargaining agreement between the players and owners after the current one expires after the 2026 season could greatly complicate the chances of an expansion. Lord knows what affect a potential lockout and strike could have.

But in truth, as much as any metropolitan area in the country, Austin deserves to be high on the waiting list of teams eager to become the first baseball additions since MLB grew to 30 with the Arizona Diamondbacks and Tampa Bay Rays in 1998.

Problem is, it's already well behind equally aggressive cities like Nashville, Salt Lake City and Portland, who are off to a big head start and already have in place much of the mechanism needed to speed along their cases.

Mackowiak has put together a leadership team and can count on advice from friend **Bob DuPuy** , a 78-year-old Florida lawyer and former president and chief operating officer for MLB for 12 years.

The smart money would say Austin's a dark horse at this stage, its broad appeal notwithstanding.

The nation's 13th-largest city, according to the U.S. Census Bureau, remains a robust, viable metro area with more than 2.4 million people, boasts a thriving, diverse economy heavily invested into sports and music and entertainment and ranks as a top-32 cable television market. What else could MLB want?

It's still the largest city in the country without a top-four professional sports league -- no MLB, NFL, NBA or NHL team -- and located far enough from Arlington and Houston not to directly overlap fan bases. That said, the feeling is the Astros and Rangers would more likely be impediments than supporters of Austin's cause. Mackowiak's group has reached out to both teams without success.

Representatives for Astros owner**Jim Crane** and the Rangers' **Ray Davis** declined to comment for this story.

"I think Austin is a terrific candidate when there is expansion," DuPuy told the Chronicle this week. "But given there hasn't been a timeline or a formal process established yet, it's tough to talk about favorites and dark horses. It's like predicting who's going to win the 2028 Kentucky Derby because the horses haven't been born yet. But I see no reason Austin couldn't come in and blow other cities out of the water."

Make no mistake. It could easily be a legitimate horse race even if Nashville and others might have the pole position.

Mackowiak points to Central Texas as one of the fastest-growing urban areas in the nation and said projections suggest Austin could surge to a population of 4.5 million in two years and maybe even six million in the next decade. The entire state of Utah has only 3.5 million people.

"I'd say we're possibly a decade or two away from being the largest state in the country," Fox said. "California has five major-league teams and 39 million people. We've got only two teams and 30 million people. We're by far the largest urban area in the U.S. that isn't served by major-league baseball."

Declining television revenue and an aging fan base demographic could also enhance the chances of expansion, which sources say could amount to a $2 billion or greater fee for each new team shared equally by the 30 teams. An extra $260 million for two new clubs could offset any loss of TV money.

ESPN opted out of its current television contract in which teams were averaging $550 million and will end its agreement after this season even though it had three years left on the deal. Manfred has reportedly had talks with NBC as well as streaming services but would like to nationalize local rights and sell them as a package.

**Competition for Rangers and Astros**

The guess here is the Rangers and Astros likely wouldn't support a third team in Texas. They own the territorial rights to a five-state region including Texas, but MLB would need only a 75% approval from ownership, and 28 other owners might like the idea of "free money" with two new teams.

Besides, the Rangers and Astros have each won a World Series in the past three seasons, and Austin could argue two more intrastate rivalries including an Austin team might fuel more ticket sales and ratings boosts.

Houston's won two World Series in the last eight years and almost two others. Texas won its only one in 2023 and would have had two, but for a certain line drive in 2011 Nelson Cruz does not want to remember.

"Regional rivalries are so much fun," Byington said. "What's better than a three-team regional rivalry? One plus one plus one equals five. Plus when they change divisions, people don't even have to fly to these games. That's the glue that pulls it all together."

DuPuy echoes that sentiment, saying, "I think you can argue it would enhance the rivalries the teams already have."

Some have suggested Austin and San Antonio team up for a franchise and maybe even put the stadium in between in New Braunfels or San Marcos, but would it even be either city's team?

"There's something to be said for belonging to a community," DuPuy said. "But you could do outreach. Have the team in Austin 70 miles away but have a San Antonio weekend or a ticket promotion and try to establish a fan base in San Antonio to come over. There are some advantages to (co-existing with the two cities), but it'd also be a little bit loss of identity."

Short of expansion, maybe Austin could lure an existing franchise to Central Texas. The Oakland A's have been dangled out there seemingly forever but we presume will find a home in Las Vegas eventually. The Miami Marlins hardly have a rush through their turnstiles, and Tampa could use another roof, if not a new home.

The chances of landing an existing team?

"I'd say 100%," Fox said without naming names. "There's a lot of disparity in baseball right now. The Dodgers are in a position of power. It's the Dodgers and everyone else. There's a lot of disparity in media rights and royalties. I think MLB is in need of a reshuffle as far as the way teams are covered. It needs a shot in the arm when it comes to the culture. I think baseball needs markets like Austin. Those few markets where the game isn't succeeding, is it better to hand it off to a market that is wealthy and hungry?"

As for the NBA, all three Texas franchises have done nicely, winning titles -- and lotteries -- and have combined to win eight titles in the past 31 years.

As for a CBA as a potential hurdle, the owners could dangle the idea of two new teams and 80 additional jobs with the 40-man rosters for two clubs as incentive to come to the table.

But history would suggest the two sides won't even begin negotiations until after the 2026 All-Star break and probably not have serious daily talks until urgency sets in once the season ends.

Mackowiak and Fox created the Austin Baseball Commission, which has a website, and has raised over $435,000 in seed money in the last month. The group has yet to meet with Manfred but was advised not to until it found a willing lead investor.

They're thinking in terms of a 40,000-seat stadium with a retractable roof with an estimated price tag of $1.5 billion.

"None of us are baseball insiders," Mackowiak said. "Nashville's an amazing city that's booming, but they already have pro football and hockey. Baseball would divide their core fan base by a third. Salt Lake City is a third of our size and has (the NBA, NHL and MLS), but are we going to let Salt Lake beat us by not getting our act together?"

**Finding an owner**

The biggest hurdle for Austin? Without question, it's the absence of a lead investor.

Of course, you can't throw a rock in the 512 without hitting about 10 mega-celebrities in what has become the hideaway spot for Hollywood's most glamourous.

Austin's most famous citizen, Rocket Man **Elon Musk** , seems to be more concerned with trimming fat these days than taking on new projects.**Matthew McConaughey** has his Horns up and a minority stake in Austin FC soccer but I've never seen him at Disch-Falk Field.

**Michael Dell** , UT's most famous dropout, has been highly supportive of the Longhorns and sports in general. He sponsored the wildly successful Dell Technology World Championship Match Play pro golf tournament at Austin Country Club from 2016-23, where aside from the COVID year the event packed in daily crowds upwards of 10,000 when Tiger Woods competed.

Longhorn football coach **Steve Sarkisian** makes a cool $10.8 mil now and may need a place to invest his dough. **Sean Miller** , the Horns' new hoops maestro, came from Xavier, a stone's throw from the Cincinnati Reds. What about Longhorn celebs like **Scottie Scheffler** and **Jordan Spieth** and **Kevin Durant** and MLB pitcher**Greg Swindell**? Doesn't**Thomas J. Henry** need some publicity? Haven't seen one of his ads in 20 minutes.

"The lead investor is a big hurdle," Byington said. "But we're doing everything in sequencing. It's a huge elephant we're trying to eat, and we're doing it bite by bite."

Austin does boast at least 10 billionaires on the Forbes 100 Richest People in the world, but Mackowiak has been told the actual number is closer to 60, many of whom hide their wealth and don't flaunt it. Mackowiak said his group has focused on "20 to 30" possibilities with the deep pockets capable of filling that role. One-third of the necessary capital in liquid form would be required to back the bid.

Heck, President Trump once desperately wanted an NFL franchise. Doesn't he like baseball? I think he threw out the first insult at the Washington Nationals opener.

"We think 15 people in the Austin area could be that lead (investor), but we're not limiting it to Austin," Mackowiak said, noting that person would have to be willing to be visible and be head-over-heels passionate about baseball. "It could even be someone from Houston or West Texas. We're working hard to secure that person."

The trio remains confident.

"Strangely," Byington said, "it doesn't keep me up at night. Baseball is like buying a house. They tend not to lose their economic value and steadily creep up in value. It's a pretty decent and secure investment for wealthy folks."

Mackowiak believes the success of the MLS franchise in Austin could pave the way for an expansion baseball team by demonstrating to MLB that the area will support sports beyond the Longhorns.

The Austin FC soccer team took the city by storm in 2021 and has technically sold out all 77 home games -- please ignore those empty seats -- at aesthetically beautiful Q2 Stadium in north Austin despite middling success on the pitch, even with an appearance in the Western Conference final after a seventh-place regular-season finish in 2022. That's been its only playoff run in four years.

"I love MLS, but the interest in baseball is 10 times that," Mackowiak said.

Maybe Austin would be hard-pressed to find a baseball diamond in the vicinity of Q2, but Mackowiak has circled five sites east of town. Downtown would be optimal, but Austin FC couldn't make it work and traffic gridlock would make a site off Zilker Park a non-starter.

Neither would the former home of the Austin American-Statesman off South Congress work. The neighborhoods would never have it. Plus, there's not the available real estate surrounding it to commercialize the venture.

The trio has the Atlanta Braves' successful Battery sports and entertainment district far north of downtown in mind when they thinks of a new ballpark for Austin.

Byington mentions the popular Gaslamp Quarter that borders Petco Park in San Diego and suggests surrounding real estate and economic development will be the key for Austin so they don't plan on asking the taxpayers to pony up anything.

"It's time," Mackowiak said. "All the reasons they had in the past don't apply."

Austin wasn't big enough. Weren't enough people with the purse strings. UT would fight it.

None of those are valid excuses any more. Even UT embraced the arrival of Major League Soccer.

Mackowiak thinks there are five boxes that must be checked for Austin to have a shot.

Priority one is to be liquid with the amount needed at the opportune time. The investor has to like baseball more than he or she likes sports. The owner would have to have longtime ties to Austin or firmly believe in the city. With 81 home games each season, the owner can't hide from the press or the fan base.

"I think 20 of (potential owners) are really quiet here and maybe not willing to take part," Mackowiak said. "There might be a top four (in reality). That'd be the closest thing to royalty we have in Austin."

The Austin Baseball Commission needs to order a deep market study to research real estate opportunities and economic factors. They'd spend $300,000 in the first week.

"We'd be off and running close to Labor Day," he said, "and we'd have our lead investor by the end of the year."

All three co-founders speak to the possibility that bringing baseball to Austin would "legitimize" it as a world-class city, but that's missing the obvious.

Austin's already that. But baseball would certainly enhance the Live Music Capital of the World. Consider an MLB team in the area an expensive add-on to the most beautiful house on the block. Trust me, no one would say there goes the neighborhood.

"It's like **Casey Stengel** said at the end of his career with the Mets," Fox said. "When he was asked what'd he say to the youth of America, he said 'Here is an opportunity, but they'd reply how much are you going to pay me? ' I like to tell people here is an opportunity. We don't need to sell it. We just need to present it."

And it's up to Major League Baseball to grab it." [Houston Chronicle](https://substack.com/redirect/1a5f673d-3571-432e-b308-65f038176aa2?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) ($)

"**Although scores of House bills died at midnight Thursday, some of them may find new life** ," Austin American-Statesman's **John C. Moritz** \-- "Just after the clock struck midnight as Thursday night faded into Friday morning, the Texas House morphed into something of a graveyard.

Perhaps "a salvage yard" would be a more accurate way to describe it.

First, a little background. Thursday -- Day 122 of the 140-day legislative session -- was the last day for bills that originated in the House to be considered on first reading by that chamber under the rules its members agreed to in January.

But the upshot was that Thursday's calendar was crammed with more than 400 bills the 150-member House did not take up earlier in the session. Given that the House gaveled in at 10 a.m., it was a given that scores of those bills would be stuck in the queue by the time the floor session would expire 14 hours later no matter how fast House managers might try to ram them through.

And, as it turned out, there wasn't a whole lot of ramming and jamming for the first 13 1/2 hours of the round-robin series of floor debates. In fact, the opposite was the case much of the time. That's because for nearly every bill that some members want to pass, there's often a passel of others who want it to die.

So, with a hard and fast deadline looming, one of the best ways to kill bills is to kill time. As bills were called up for debate, members lined up to ask questions about them, and the questions were often laden with histrionic and hyperbolic language. And sometimes, the questions were lighthearted. The practice is known in legislative parlance as "chubbing."

One question about putting more state regulations on food trucks, prompted Democratic state Rep.**Terry Canales** of Edinburg to ask questions of Republican state Rep. **Brooks Landgraf** of Odessa, the author of [House Bill 2248](https://substack.com/redirect/96b877d3-87da-47e5-9e6b-64265fcef8a4?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg), in Spanish. And Landgraf responded in kind.

The measure passed by a comfortable 86-31 margin, but it took up time.

But another bill, HB 3520 by Rep. **David Spiller** , R-Jacksboro, to lessen the amount of liability insurance coverage rideshare drivers need to carry, took up a whole lot more time. It came up for debate about 12 hours into the 14-hour floor session as one member after another drilled down into the minutiae of insurance law and of the rights of injured riders to be adequately compensated if a rideshare driver causes a crash.

That went on for about a half-hour, and nearly that much more time passed while the House parliamentarian studied whether one member's effort to kill the bill because of a procedural error was legally sound. Turns out, it wasn't, and the bill cruised to passage 85-32.

Throughout most of the chubbing and time-killing, House members for the most part remained eerily calm as the minutes and hours slipped away. That changed at 11:30 p.m. when the gallery began filling up with legislative staffers and lobbyists, which injected a charge of adrenaline into the members who had by then already struggled through back-to-back-to-back workdays that had stretched deep into the night.

House Speaker **Dustin Burrows** accelerated his pace as he called bill after bill to the floor. The reading clerks morphed into auctioneers as they provided one-sentence descriptions of each piece of legislation.

Through it all, members urged them to go even faster and they hooted and cheered with the passage of each new bill. When the ornate clock mounted on the wall just below the section of the second-floor gallery that faces the speaker's rostrum hit the 11:55 p.m. mark, the pace was breakneck.

Members passed three bills in the final five minutes. They tried for a fourth but midnight struck before there was time to vote.

The last day's calendar was not a strategic list of must-pass bills, but actually a hodgepodge collection topped by leftovers. And those leftovers were there because many of the bills that the House had hoped to get to on Monday ended up being pushed to Tuesday because the chamber had run out of time.

And many of Tuesday's bills were pushed to Wednesday, meaning many of the proposals scheduled for Wednesday topped the list on Thursday. But once Thursday arrived, there would be no tomorrow to continue considering House bills.

But for all the bills that died with the stroke of midnight -- and there were about 200 of them -- many of them might actually remain on life support. And that's where the "salvage yard" comparison comes back into play.

Bills that die in one chamber can be revived as "spare parts" for bills that originated in the other chamber. If the intent of both pieces of legislation is roughly the same, the moribund measures can be tacked on as an amendment to the bill that's still breathing.

And there's still time. The House has until May 27 to take up Senate bills. And that means it might still be too soon to dig the graves of all the bills that didn't make Thursday's deadline. But the one drop-date that is absolutely certain is June 2. That's when the 2025 session must come to an end." [AAS](https://substack.com/redirect/6c3f145a-74b9-4b6e-b530-7622fc9eccab?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) ($)

"**Texas Senate reveals $8 billion school funding plan as it seeks middle ground with House** ," Texas Tribune's **Jaden Ellison** \-- "The Texas Senate on Thursday unveiled its highly anticipated counterproposal to the House's multibillion-dollar public school funding legislation, which would establish a long-term teacher pay raise system tied to years of experience, phase out the state's reliance on untrained teachers by the end of the decade and overhaul the state's special education funding system.

Sen. **Brandon Creighton** , who chairs the Senate's education committee, called his version of House Bill 2, an $8 billion investment into Texas public schools, "exciting and historic," adding that the bill would encompass more funding "than any public education package that we've ever passed."

"Every single district, from Beaumont to El Paso, from Wichita Falls to McAllen, benefits," the Conroe Republican said during a public hearing for the legislation on Thursday.

The bill is the result of ongoing negotiations between leaders in the House and Senate who have worked to find middle ground between their diverging funding proposals as the end of the 2025 legislative session approaches. The Senate took many of the education bills it passed earlier in the session and combined them into a sweeping 225-page proposal.

During early parts of Thursday's hearing, legislators and members of the public highlighted two notable deviations in the Senate bill from the House's nearly $8 billion proposal: a modest $55 per-student increase to public schools' base funding and the absence of millions in funding for fine arts.

House lawmakers' version of the bill sought to boost schools' base money, known as the basic allotment, from $6,160 to $6,555 per student, with future increases tied to property values. They also reserved $15 million per year for students in grades 6-12 enrolled in courses like art and music.

The Senate's version of HB 2 does not include additional funding the House set aside for students in bilingual education programs and leaves out a provision that would extend free prekindergarten to children with disabilities.

Senate Democrats during the meeting also expressed concerns about the revised legislation imposing certain mandates on traditional public schools that do not apply to charter schools, which are publicly funded but privately managed.

Under both the House and Senate proposals, for example, only traditional public schools would be required to keep educators without formal training from teaching core subjects and to notify parents when their child has an uncertified instructor.

"With all due respect, sometimes it feels like we call charter schools public schools when it's convenient, when we're helping, but we exclude them from the public school requirements," said Sen. **Jos e Menendez**, D-San Antonio.

Among the roughly six dozen Texans who signed up to testify on the Senate's version of HB 2, many spoke in favor of the bill's overall investment in public education. But they raised worries about its proposal to only increase districts' base funding by $55 per student, which they said could potentially leave their schools and staff without adequate support." [Texas Tribune](https://substack.com/redirect/b46e582f-f1eb-43ba-a0ba-2f5715ce7e81?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)

"**What a $500M idea could mean for Texas ' film and TV industry**," Dallas Morning News' **Uwe Ede-Osifo** \-- "When prolific TV show creator and Fort Worth native **Taylor Sheridan** appeared before Texas lawmakers last fall to [lobby](https://substack.com/redirect/931c2583-9985-4af7-b53b-b50853a2b96f?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) for film incentives, he bemoaned how productions were being lured away to locales that boasted higher media rebates or tax credits.

"One of my great frustrations is that I wrote a movie called _[Hell or High Water](https://substack.com/redirect/606d7506-1437-47f8-9aeb-c90786b5b0ea?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)_[,](https://substack.com/redirect/606d7506-1437-47f8-9aeb-c90786b5b0ea?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)__ which was really my love poem to my state," Sheridan said, "and they filmed the darn thing in New Mexico. They could not ignore the 30% rebate that New Mexico offered."

Now, months later, a bipartisan bill with star power behind it -- **Matthew McConaughey** and **Woody Harrelson** have been advocates -- is advancing through [the Legislature](https://substack.com/redirect/8900af93-ea81-41d4-96ca-050799407661?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) and may bolster Texas as a formidable filming site.

Senate Bill 22 would establish a biennial fund of $500 million for 10 years, a record-breaking amount for [the Texas Moving Image Industry Incentive Program](https://substack.com/redirect/bbe91ddd-88f3-4100-b320-97339acecf4b?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg), which legislators previously [boosted](https://substack.com/redirect/a368aa19-4816-4a77-89db-22e017addccd?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) with $200 million in 2023. It would also loosen some application requirements and offer studios and investors a sense of security with the 10-year runway.

The Senate passed the bill in April, and it awaits a House vote. The last day for Senate bills to get a first vote on the House floor is May 27.

Lawmakers must also approve the funding for the bill in the state's 2026-27 spending plan. The budget is in negotiations between the House and Senate, and a compromise is expected to pass at the end of May. Both versions of the bill include funding for the program, which Lt. Gov. **Dan Patrick** , a Republican, has named among his priorities for the session.

In Texas, incentives essentially work as reimbursements for productions based on how much money they spend in the state. The more money spent, the higher the potential rewards.

The grants are not a one-way transaction or handout, the bill's proponents say, pointing to the trickle-down economic activity spurred by productions.

Sheridan's _Yellowstone_ spin-off _[1883](https://substack.com/redirect/ec6204d6-c452-4789-ae77-81feb18679e0?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)_ , for example,__ spent roughly $440 million filming in 2021 throughout Fort Worth, a figure that encompasses transportation, hospitality, catering and security costs, according to the city's film commissioner, **Taylor Hardy**.

Supporters of the bill have also argued it would bring talent back to the state.

"More Texans who left Texas and went to other incentivized states for work, because there wasn't enough work here, are going to move back," McConaughey, the Texas-born-and-raised actor, told the Senate Finance Committee in March. "Because one, they'd rather live here, and two, now they can work here."

He and Harrelson also pushed for the incentives in [an ad](https://substack.com/redirect/6ab3d5b6-197c-4d12-aa1a-d39e0cda9156?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) with fellow Texas actors **Dennis Quaid** and **Ren ee Zellweger**.

The bill specifies funding would not go toward shows or films with "inappropriate" material or material that depicts Texas negatively. Some Republicans have voiced concern that the program could still fund projects with profane language. Some conservatives have also opposed using taxpayer money to subsidize productions.

"They could make movies about William F. Buckley [a conservative commentator and public figure], and I'd oppose it," Rep.**Briscoe Cain** , R-Deer Park, [posted](https://substack.com/redirect/55f3e47f-4b90-4bc6-b1ae-24719141a150?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) on X in March.

Legislators continue to hash out the fine print of the proposal in Austin, but here in North Texas, where the so-called Sheridanverse and [biblical drama](https://substack.com/redirect/04b79d28-2a19-4ea7-aa84-63ee57adb68e?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) _The Chosen_ both film, industry insiders are generally optimistic about the future.

"We're going to have a surge in productions," Hardy said." [DMN](https://substack.com/redirect/e405706a-0573-44cd-af61-93949d29af70?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) ($)

"**Without public input, Texas House committee advances bill to end State Fair 's gun ban**," Dallas Morning News' **Nolan D. McCaskill** \-- "A proposal that takes aim at the State Fair of Texas' gun ban advanced out of a House committee Friday without a public hearing that typically precedes such votes.

Only elected, appointed or employed peace officers are permitted to bring firearms to the State Fair following a 2023 shooting that injured three fairgoers.

The [Senate passed the bill in April](https://substack.com/redirect/2fe1489b-5b87-4758-ac67-1c98814acb0f?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg). It would prevent contractors from banning guns in government-owned public spaces. The State Fair contracts with Dallas annually to use Fair Park.

Residents or licensed gun owners could file complaints with the attorney general if they suspect a contractor is violating the law, according to the proposal. Violators would face a potential civil penalty of $1,000 to $1,500 for the first violation and up to $10,500 for subsequent violations.

The [Texas Supreme Court last year denied](https://substack.com/redirect/a5f1742c-2eea-4479-8d4f-c26b18fe89df?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) Attorney General **Ken Paxton 's **effort to stop the ban. Paxton petitioned the court to block the ban after unfavorable rulings from a Democratic Dallas County district judge and an appeals court with three GOP justices.

The House received the bill from the Senate on May 1 and referred it to the State Affairs Committee. Traditionally, legislation is laid out at a public hearing, where lawmakers discuss the proposal and Texas citizens can weigh in.

But the panel skipped that crucial part of the process and voted 9-5 to advance it. The rushed meeting came as the House faces a May 27 deadline to vote on Senate bills.

While technically open to the public, the vote -- which was not streamed online -- occurred behind closed doors in a noon meeting that was noticed at 11 a.m. without a detailed agenda." [DMN](https://substack.com/redirect/059ac27e-7293-48c8-831a-7eaf9e150680?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) ($)
* * *
* **LOCAL GOVERNMENT**

"**Economic development and tourism CEOs exiting, new mayor coming in at rocky time for San Antonio** ," San Antonio Express-News' **Madison Iszler** \-- "Ask the average San Antonio resident to rattle off the names of the CEOs of [Greater: SATX](https://substack.com/redirect/49a76738-4de9-4c0b-8b32-677e68f339e4?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) and [Visit San Antonio](https://substack.com/redirect/dc7420c1-f95f-4efe-a69f-fdc9494de6bf?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) and they'll probably give you a blank stare.

You might have more luck asking them who the mayor is, though low voter turnout in the recent [primary election](https://substack.com/redirect/3ba56bc4-1804-4cf4-b464-88b65f4400c8?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) among 27 candidates might be an indication residents don't care who's at the city's helm, either.

But those three leaders play key roles in shaping how attractive San Antonio is to companies, travelers and residents -- convincing businesses to build facilities and bring jobs here, persuading tourists to spend their money here, and advocating for major projects such as a creating a downtown sports and entertainment district and expanding San Antonio International Airport.

Two of those positions are currently filled with interim appointees. Marc Anderson left Visit San Antonio in late April and Jenna Saucedo-Herrera exited Greater: SATX in mid-May.

**Gina Ortiz Jones** and **Rolando Pablos** -- both City Hall outsiders -- are jockeying to replace outgoing Mayor **Ron Nirenberg** in a June 7 runoff.

The leadership shake-up comes as the city's economy is entering a tumultuous period.

**Big issues**

On-again, off-again tariffs sparking worries about a trade war are creating uncertainty for many companies, including automotive manufacturers and parts suppliers on San Antonio's South Side. Some could opt to "reshore" operations -- moving lower-cost plants in foreign countries to the United States -- while others could hold off on investments and hiring until they have more clarity.

Largely because of headwinds caused by the Trump administration's trade policies, property, sales and hotel occupancy tax revenues are down, as is the amount the city collects annually from its airport and residential and commercial construction permits. The next mayor and City Council [will have to cut millions of dollars in spending this summer](https://substack.com/redirect/d9dd8409-da51-47d1-852d-b97c2c4e5dcc?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) to avoid red ink.

The Trump administration is also throwing thousands of federal employees out of their jobs and slashing federal funding, which is leaving nonprofits struggling to make up for shortfalls and quashing research.

Then there's "Project Marvel," city officials' code name for the multi-billion-dollar sports and entertainment district they've proposed in the urban core. Left to answer are critical questions about who should pay for some of its components, which include a new arena for the Spurs, a larger Convention Center, a renovated Alamodome and a second Convention Center hotel, and how much public money to put on the line.

San Antonio International Airport is in the midst of a 20-year expansion and seeking to draw more nonstop flights, investments that could make the region more appealing to companies interested in moving to or expanding in San Antonio.

This mix of threats and opportunities would be challenging for any longtime economic-development leader -- whose job is to stay in continuous talks with the area's major employers, government officials, trade and industry groups, and major companies around the country looking to expand or relocation.

The departures at Greater: SATX and Visit San Antonio and the pending election of a new mayor with no City Hall experience make it harder to meet the dangers to the local economy head-on or to exploit the opportunities.

But**Jeff Webster** , president and CEO of the Greater San Antonio Chamber of Commerce, looks at the situation from a slightly different perspective.

"There's great talent changing spots. Any time you have that, it's an opportunity to refocus, redirect, get your priorities in line and re-attack," he said." TOP NEWS "Relatively quiet Jeep Weekend is wrapping up Sunday after 275 arrests amid massive police patrol ," Houston Chronicle's Caroline Wilburn \-- "Almost 300 people have been arrested on Bolivar Peninsula since police put into effect their Jeep Weekend enforcement plan Tuesday. On Sunday, as , the Galveston County Sheriff's Office said a total of 275 people had been arrested and 42 guns recovered over the past six days, according to spokesperson Zach Davidson. The Harris County Sheriff's Office, which had been helping manage the event since Friday, began breaking down its equipment and packing up on Sunday. The sheriff's office didn't immediately respond to a request for comment. Beaches along Bolivar Peninsula were mostly deserted Saturday, the as Galveston County Sheriff Jimmy Fullen looked to put an end to the typically rowdy and chaotic event. More than a dozen agencies aided in patrolling the peninsula's beaches and Texas State Highway 87, where police checkpoints were set up to keep guns and violence off the beach. "If you control the traffic you control the whole event and that's exactly what we did," Fullen said Saturday. He made security during Jeep Weekend a key part of his campaign run last year, vowing to put an end to the violence and chaos of previous years. It's not yet known how many people made the trip to Bolivar Peninsula for this year's event. But estimates put last year's crowd at about 100,000."
